description: Commelina erecta, commonly known as white mouth dayflower, slender dayflower, or widow's tears. C. erecta is a blue flower often recognized by its two large blue petals and smaller white petal. It is a perennial herb common across the world and native throughout the Americas, Africa and western Asia.

care.toxicity_note: Toxic to Dogs, Toxic to Cats, Toxic to Horses. Signs: Dermatitis
common_problems: Too much direct sun can cause leaf scorch.
Soggy and poorly drained soils can cause root rot.
In cool and humid conditions, botrytis blight can occur causing a gray mold to grow on the leaves and flowers.
Some species can be invasive.
Choose the best variety for your climate and be aware of any invasive potential if used as a mass planting.
common_pests: Monitor for spider mites, aphids, thrips, and mealybugs.
Snails and caterpillars can damage young shoots.
care.toxicity_first_aid: Remove any remaining plant material from mouth. Rinse with water. Monitor for symptoms (vomiting, drooling, lethargy). Call poison control or vet if symptoms appear.
care.used_for_details:
(NC State ethnobotany) Native
American tribes used Virginia spiderwort for various
food and medicinal purposes.

description: The fruits are a light green capsule that displays from May to August.  They are surrounded by 3 green bracts and will mature 2-3 weeks after flowering. The mature capsule splits into 3-valved sections. Each capsule is 4 to 6 mm in size and releases 3 to 6 brown seeds.
origin: Ontario to Central and Eastern United States

care.light_preferred: Bright indirect light, no direct sun
care.light_guide: NC State: Deep shade (Less than 2 hours to no direct sunlight)
care.tips: Similar plants: Trillium pusillum
common_problems: This plant has no serious insects or disease issues.
Clumps can become quite large; division may be necessary.
By mid-summer, the foliage can become somewhat unattractive due to its tendency to sprawl.
common_pests: This plant has no serious insects or disease issues.
Snails sometimes damage the young shoots, and deer and rabbits occasionally eat the foliage.
care.edible_parts: The leaves and stems are edible, fresh or cooked.

description: Tradescantia pallida is a species of spiderwort native to the Gulf Coast region of eastern Mexico. It is a perennial herbaceous species with a trailing habit. The cultivar T. pallida 'Purpurea', commonly called purple heart or purple queen, is widely grown as a houseplant, outdoor container plant, or a garden groundcover. The species has been proven useful in indicating and removing air and soil pollutants and has also been used in food technology.
origin: Mexico

care.light_preferred: Bright indirect light, no direct sun
care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)
care.tips: Similar plants: Curio repens
common_problems: Protect the plant from strong winds that can damage the stems.
It has a tendency to become invasive in beds.
common_pests: Monitor for scale, aphids, vine weevil, and mealybugs.
Outdoor plants can be eaten on by caterpillars, slugs and snails.

description: The fruit is a 2 seeded elongated dry capsule that measures less than 0.5 inches long and appears after the flowers bloom. The seeds are believed to be dispersed by the wind.
origin: Southern Mexico to Guatemala

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)
care.tips: Similar plants: Anthurium
common_problems: Overwatering can cause root rot.

common_pests: Monitor for mealybugs, whiteflies, and spider mites.

description: After flowering, 3-chambered seed capsules are produced and are 0.25 inches long.  Each contains several seeds. The seeds are 2-3 mm long, flat, and ovoid shaped.
origin: East Central and Eastern United States

care.tips: Similar plants: Arisaema triphyllum
common_problems: The Virginia spiderwort does not have any serious insect or disease issues.
By summer, the foliage has a sprawling habit and is less attractive.
This species can spread easily by seed and may need to be controlled.
common_pests: The Virginia spiderwort does not have any serious insect or disease issues.
Snails can sometimes damage the new growth.
care.used_for_details: (NC State ethnobotany) Native Americans used the plant for food and medical purposes.
care.edible_parts: Leaves can be eaten raw mixed in salads. Teas can be made from the plant.

description: Tradescantia zanonia, formerly known as Campelia zanonia, is an evergreen perennial subshrub of the dayflower family, closely related to Tradescantia zebrina. It is native to the tropical Americas, ranging from Mexico through Central America and the Caribbean to Bolivia and southern Brazil. Its common names include cañagria, cascajo flor, cinta, rascadera, and suelda.
origin: Canada, U.S.A., South America

care.light_preferred: Bright indirect light, no direct sun
care.light_guide: NC State: Dappled Sunlight (Shade through upper canopy all day)
care.tips: Similar plants: Euphorbia hypericifolia Breathless™ Group
common_problems: Too much direct sun can cause leaf scorch.
Soggy and poorly drained soils can cause root rot.
In cool and humid conditions, botrytis blight can occur causing a gray mold to grow on the leaves and flowers.
Some species can be invasive.
Choose the best variety for your climate and be aware of any invasive potential if used as a mass planting.
common_pests: Monitor for spider mites, aphids, thrips, and mealybugs.
Snails and caterpillars can damage young shoots.
care.used_for_details:
(NC State ethnobotany) Native
American tribes used Virginia spiderwort for various
food and medicinal purposes.

description: The fruit is capsule containing grayish-brown seeds.
origin: Mexico to Columbia

care.light_preferred: Bright indirect light, no direct sun
care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)
care.tips: Similar plants: Asparagus setaceus
common_problems: Root rot and stem rot can occur in soggy soil.
Leaf spot or other fungal diseases can result from overcrowding or from getting the leaves wet during watering.
common_pests: Monitor for aphids, mealybugs, scale, whiteflies and spider mites as these can be an issue if left unchecked.
